description:
You are helping a capacity–planning engineer who has pulled a
    “wide” tab-separated resource report and now needs a slimmed-down
    version for a weekly slide deck.  Everything must be done only with
    tools that already exist in a typical minimal Linux installation
    (e.g., cut, paste, tail, head, printf, cat, mkdir).

    Current situation
    -----------------
    • A data file already exists at
      /home/user/data/usage_report.tsv  
      It is TAB-delimited and contains seven columns with a single
      header row.

    Required end state
    ------------------
    1. Create a new directory called
       /home/user/analysis  
       if it is not already present.

    2. Produce a trimmed report that contains **only** the three
       columns listed below, in this exact order, **without** the
       original header row:
         • column 2 → ServerID
         • column 3 → CPU_Usage
         • column 4 → Mem_Usage

    3. Add a brand-new one-line header to the very top of the trimmed
       data.  The header must be **exactly** (TABs, spelling and
       punctuation included, no leading/trailing spaces):

           ServerID<TAB>CPU_Usage(%)<TAB>Mem_Usage(%)

       (The “<TAB>” above is just to show where TAB characters must go;
       the real file must contain real TAB byte 0x09 characters.)

    4. Save the final, header-inclusive, TAB-delimited file as
       /home/user/analysis/weekly_capacity.tsv

       Format rules that **will** be automatically checked:
         • File exists.
         • Exactly 6 lines total (1 header + 5 data lines).
         • Every line ends with a single newline (LF, 0x0A).
         • Data lines appear in the same order as in the source file.
         • Numeric fields keep their original precision (one
           fractional digit as supplied).

    5. For auditability, create a plain-text log file at
       /home/user/analysis/capacity_extraction.log

       Logging requirements:
         a) One log line per interactive terminal command you execute
            to achieve the transformation (command lines only, no
            prompts or extra commentary).
         b) Each line must begin with a timestamp in 24-hour
            HH:MM:SS format, followed by exactly one ASCII space, then
            the command that was run.  Example pattern (do **NOT** copy
            literally, the time is illustrative):

                14:07:03 cut -f2,3,4 /home/user/data/usage_report.tsv

         c) At least four log lines are expected.
         d) No blank lines before, after or between the entries.

    6. Make sure all created files are world-readable (the automated
       tests will run as an unprivileged user).

    Hints (do not print the commands themselves):
       • The cut utility can slice columns; tail can skip the header;
         paste can combine intermediate results if you prefer a two-cut
         workflow.
       • A single command pipeline is acceptable as long as it is
         logged as one full line in the audit log.
       • Use $(date +%T) or the `date` builtin to generate timestamps.

    Deliverables the grader will look for:
       • /home/user/analysis/weekly_capacity.tsv – exact content &
         format as specified.
       • /home/user/analysis/capacity_extraction.log – exists, contains
         ≥4 timestamped command lines, each line matching the regular
         expression:  ^[0-2][0-9]:[0-5][0-9]:[0-5][0-9] .+

    Remember: do not simply describe what you would do—actually perform
    the steps in the terminal so the files are physically present when
    grading begins.
truth:
Initial fixture file (already present before the agent starts):
    Path: /home/user/data/usage_report.tsv  
    Exact content (TAB-separated, \n = LF newline):

        Timestamp<TAB>ServerID<TAB>CPU_Usage<TAB>Mem_Usage<TAB>Disk_Usage<TAB>Network_In<TAB>Network_Out\n
        2023-07-01T00:15:00Z<TAB>srv-001<TAB>52.5<TAB>68.2<TAB>70.1<TAB>120.4<TAB>98.6\n
        2023-07-01T00:15:00Z<TAB>srv-002<TAB>33.0<TAB>44.5<TAB>60.0<TAB>80.2<TAB>60.0\n
        2023-07-01T00:15:00Z<TAB>srv-003<TAB>85.2<TAB>90.1<TAB>88.5<TAB>200.1<TAB>150.2\n
        2023-07-01T00:15:00Z<TAB>srv-004<TAB>10.5<TAB>25.3<TAB>30.5<TAB>40.4<TAB>30.1\n
        2023-07-01T00:15:00Z<TAB>srv-005<TAB>67.7<TAB>75.0<TAB>79.2<TAB>130.2<TAB>110.7\n

    Expected final file #1:
    Path: /home/user/analysis/weekly_capacity.tsv  
    Content (TAB-separated, final newline after last line):

        ServerID<TAB>CPU_Usage(%)<TAB>Mem_Usage(%)\n
        srv-001<TAB>52.5<TAB>68.2\n
        srv-002<TAB>33.0<TAB>44.5\n
        srv-003<TAB>85.2<TAB>90.1\n
        srv-004<TAB>10.5<TAB>25.3\n
        srv-005<TAB>67.7<TAB>75.0\n

    Expected final file #2:
    Path: /home/user/analysis/capacity_extraction.log  
    Validation rule:
        • File must exist.
        • Size must be > 0 bytes.
        • Each line must match regex:
              ^[0-2][0-9]:[0-5][0-9]:[0-5][0-9] .+$
        • At least 4 such lines.

    Permissions requirement for both files:
        • Mode 0644 (-rw-r--r--) or more permissive, so they are
          readable by the grading process.

    The automated test workflow will:
        1. Verify that /home/user/analysis/weekly_capacity.tsv exists
           and its exact byte content matches the block above.
        2. Verify that /home/user/analysis/capacity_extraction.log
           exists and satisfies the regex/line-count checks.
